terms.definition: Mono- or disaccharide that is rapidly digested and quickly raises blood glucose.
terms.exampleSentence: She advised the patient to limit simple carbohydrates such as sweets and sugary drinks between meals.
terms.contextNotes: Includes glucose, fructose, sucrose and lactose.

terms.definition: Polysaccharide such as starch that is digested more slowly and provides sustained energy.
terms.exampleSentence: The dietitian encouraged complex carbohydrates like wholegrain bread, oats and pulses at each main meal.
terms.contextNotes: Often paired with fibre and a lower glycaemic index.

terms.definition: Protein source that supplies all nine essential amino acids in adequate proportions.
terms.exampleSentence: Animal foods and soya are complete proteins, whereas most plant sources need to be combined.
terms.contextNotes: Vegan diets achieve completeness through complementary plant proteins.

terms.definition: Fatty acid with no double bonds, usually solid at room temperature and largely from animal sources.
terms.exampleSentence: He was advised to reduce saturated fat by trimming visible fat from meat and choosing lower-fat dairy.
terms.contextNotes: UK guidance: <11% of food energy from saturated fat.

terms.definition: Fatty acid containing one or more double bonds, typically liquid at room temperature.
terms.exampleSentence: Replacing butter with rapeseed or olive oil increases unsaturated fat intake.
terms.contextNotes: Includes both monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids.

terms.definition: Industrially or naturally occurring unsaturated fat with hydrogen atoms on opposite sides of a double bond.
terms.exampleSentence: Trans fats from partially hydrogenated oils raise LDL cholesterol and should be minimised.
terms.contextNotes: WHO targets elimination of industrially produced trans fats.

terms.definition: Fatty acid with a single carbon-carbon double bond, abundant in olive and rapeseed oils.
terms.exampleSentence: A Mediterranean-style diet is rich in monounsaturated fatty acids and is linked to better lipid profiles.
terms.contextNotes: Often abbreviated MUFA in nutrition literature.

terms.definition: Fatty acid with two or more carbon-carbon double bonds, including omega-3 and omega-6 families.
terms.exampleSentence: Oily fish such as mackerel are an important source of long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acids.
terms.contextNotes: Essential PUFAs cannot be synthesised by the body.

terms.definition: Dietary fibre that dissolves in water to form a gel and helps lower blood cholesterol.
terms.exampleSentence: Oats, barley and pulses provide soluble fibre that can modestly reduce LDL cholesterol.
terms.contextNotes: Beta-glucan from oats has an authorised cholesterol-lowering claim.

terms.definition: Dietary fibre that does not dissolve in water and adds bulk to stool, easing bowel transit.
terms.exampleSentence: Wheat bran and wholemeal bread provide insoluble fibre that can help relieve constipation.
terms.contextNotes: Adequate fluid intake is needed alongside increased insoluble fibre.

terms.definition: Fat-soluble vitamin essential for vision, immune function and epithelial integrity.
terms.exampleSentence: Liver, eggs and dairy are key dietary sources of preformed vitamin A.
terms.contextNotes: Pregnant women should avoid liver due to retinol teratogenicity.

terms.definition: Water-soluble cobalamin needed for red cell formation and neurological function.
terms.exampleSentence: Strict vegans require a reliable vitamin B12 supplement to prevent megaloblastic anaemia.
terms.contextNotes: Absorption requires intrinsic factor from gastric parietal cells.

terms.definition: Water-soluble pyridoxine involved in amino acid metabolism and neurotransmitter synthesis.
terms.exampleSentence: Vitamin B6 deficiency can present with peripheral neuropathy and microcytic anaemia.
terms.contextNotes: Long-term high doses can cause sensory neuropathy.

terms.definition: Water-soluble ascorbic acid required for collagen synthesis and antioxidant defence.
terms.exampleSentence: Citrus fruits, peppers and broccoli are excellent sources of vitamin C.
terms.contextNotes: Enhances absorption of non-haem iron when taken in the same meal.

terms.definition: Fat-soluble vitamin that regulates calcium and phosphate balance, mainly synthesised in skin.
terms.exampleSentence: A daily 10 microgram vitamin D supplement is recommended in autumn and winter in the UK.
terms.contextNotes: Status is measured as serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D.

terms.definition: Fat-soluble tocopherol acting as a major lipid-phase antioxidant in cell membranes.
terms.exampleSentence: Vegetable oils, nuts and seeds are the main dietary sources of vitamin E.
terms.contextNotes: Frank deficiency is rare except in fat malabsorption.

terms.definition: Water-soluble B-vitamin essential for DNA synthesis and red cell formation.
terms.exampleSentence: Women planning pregnancy should take 400 micrograms of folic acid daily until week 12.
terms.contextNotes: Folate is the natural form; folic acid is the synthetic supplement.

terms.definition: Iron bound to haemoglobin or myoglobin in animal foods, with high bioavailability.
terms.exampleSentence: Red meat, poultry and fish provide haem iron, which is absorbed more efficiently than the non-haem form.
terms.contextNotes: Bioavailability ~15-35% versus 2-20% for non-haem iron.

terms.definition: Plant-source or supplemental iron with lower bioavailability that is enhanced by vitamin C.
terms.exampleSentence: She advised pairing pulses with vitamin C-rich foods to improve non-haem iron absorption.
terms.contextNotes: Tea and coffee tannins inhibit non-haem iron uptake.

terms.definition: Essential mineral required for bone mineralisation, muscle contraction and nerve signalling.
terms.exampleSentence: Adults need around 700 mg of calcium daily, mainly from dairy or fortified alternatives.
terms.contextNotes: Absorption depends on adequate vitamin D status.

terms.definition: Mineral involved in muscle and nerve function, glucose control and bone structure.
terms.exampleSentence: Wholegrains, nuts and green leafy vegetables are reliable sources of magnesium.
terms.contextNotes: Severe deficiency may cause hypocalcaemia and arrhythmia.

terms.definition: Trace mineral essential for immune function, wound healing and protein synthesis.
terms.exampleSentence: Meat, shellfish and pulses are good dietary sources of zinc.
terms.contextNotes: Phytate in unrefined grains reduces zinc bioavailability.

terms.definition: Trace element required for the synthesis of thyroid hormones T3 and T4.
terms.exampleSentence: Milk, white fish and seaweed are key dietary sources of iodine in the UK.
terms.contextNotes: Deficiency in pregnancy can impair fetal neurodevelopment.

terms.definition: Trace mineral that forms part of antioxidant enzymes and supports thyroid function.
terms.exampleSentence: Brazil nuts, fish and eggs are particularly rich in selenium.
terms.contextNotes: Intake varies widely with the selenium content of soil.

terms.definition: Long-chain polyunsaturated fatty acid found mainly in oily fish and some plant oils.
terms.exampleSentence: Two portions of fish per week, including one of oily fish, supply useful omega-3 fatty acids.
terms.contextNotes: EPA and DHA are the long-chain marine forms; ALA is the plant form.

terms.definition: Mineral that regulates fluid balance and blood pressure; the main cation in extracellular fluid.
terms.exampleSentence: UK adults are advised to limit sodium to no more than 2.4 g per day, equivalent to 6 g of salt.
terms.contextNotes: Most dietary sodium comes from processed foods, not the salt cellar.

terms.definition: Major intracellular cation that supports nerve, muscle and cardiac function and blunts sodium effects.
terms.exampleSentence: Increasing potassium-rich fruit and vegetables can help lower blood pressure.
terms.contextNotes: Patients with chronic kidney disease may need potassium restriction.

terms.definition: Unit of food energy equal to 1000 calories, often labelled simply as a 'calorie' on packaging.
terms.exampleSentence: His estimated energy requirement is around 2200 kilocalories per day.
terms.contextNotes: 1 kcal = 4.184 kJ; both appear on UK food labels.

terms.definition: Ranking of carbohydrate foods by their effect on postprandial blood glucose, relative to a reference.
terms.exampleSentence: Choosing low glycaemic index carbohydrates can help smooth blood glucose in type 2 diabetes.
terms.contextNotes: GI alone does not capture portion size; consider glycaemic load.

terms.definition: Product of a food's glycaemic index and its available carbohydrate per serving.
terms.exampleSentence: Watermelon has a high glycaemic index but a relatively low glycaemic load per typical serving.
terms.contextNotes: Better predictor of postprandial glucose than GI alone.
